paragraphs.body: The “Awe and Aure” Behind Massive Statues In a news article written shortly after the completion of the new world’s tallest statue titled “A Statue of Unity at a Time of Deep Social, Political Divides,” the author, Viju Cherian argues that the Statue of Unity feels like a gimmick during India’s current Political unrest. He proposes his point by developing several important ideas about how the government used the statue as an attempt to step forward and overcome unrest through the statue. When I first learned about the Statue of Unity, I began researching the man it portrayed. Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel was an Indian statesman and a major figure in the fight for Independence from Britain in the forties. In his life he managed to unify 565 Princely states into one single India which I feel in many ways justifies the construction of this ginormous monument. Cherian points out that the Statue of Unity featuring Patel was unveiled as a symbol of pride and unity for India by citing the celebrations and events associated with its unveiling including “a fly-past by the Indian Air Force, dance, and music performances and a ‘Run for Unity’ organised in different cities across the country.” He then explains the failure of the government’s use of Patel’s likeness to bring people together by explaining the decrease in popularity that India’s prime minister, Narendra Modi, has been experiencing since 2013. In the article he provides multiple examples of Modi’s party, the Bharatiya Janata Party, causing civil and political disorder among the people of India. Cherian also expresses his thoughts that even though the statue represents an enormously powerful meaning to India as a country it will not cure its current political situation especially with the protests from local tribes of which land was taken to build the statue on. Besides his views on the politics of India, Cherian also comments on the general meaning of statues. He first implies that large statues are not nearly as impactful as they used to be. Cherian argues “The symbolism behind tall, imposing statues is not a new one — in fact, it is an old one which lacks the awe and aura it once had.”

annotations.text: When I first learned about the Statue of Unity, I began researching the man it portrayed. Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel was an Indian statesman and a major figure in the fight for Independence from Britain in the forties. In his life he managed to unify 565 Princely states into one single India which I feel in many ways justifies the construction of this ginormous monument.

paragraphs.body: In my opinion, his assessment is a little harsh. While I do agree that statues don’t hold as much meaning to us now in a modern age as they once did, I believe that the statue still holds this amazing awe factor purely based on the immense size. Standing beneath a 600-foot statue is a weird wild feeling. It makes you feel small. Looking up at towering skyscrapers is one thing. But looking up at a replica of a human figure that huge has a different effect. It makes me think about how big the world is and how insignificant I, as one singular human being, am in space and time. It makes me wonder what I can accomplish in my life. A lot of times, people think they are bigger than they are, and a massive statue can be a reality check. Sometimes people who don’t understand other cultures feel superior to them and they need to be reminded not only how big the world is but also how varied cultures across the globe are. If I had gone to India, I would not have known who Patel is despite how prominent he must be in India. To think that there’s a statue that huge of someone I don’t know anything about is interesting to me. What else do I not know about other contemporary cultures or the cultures of other time periods?

annotations.text: Standing beneath a 600-foot statue is a weird wild feeling.

paragraphs.body: When I think of Columbus, in contrast to my feeling of insignificance beneath that massive statue, I think of someone that viewed themselves as a superior being. When he landed in the Bahamas and experienced the hunter-gatherer lifestyle the natives employed, I can imagine he thought it as absurd or undeveloped. His superior perspective led to the enslavement and genocide of the natives. From the school curriculum until I was 14, I learned only a limited and non-negative coverage focusing only on Columbus’s discovery. Was that because they were trying to protect us kids from harsh realities? When I was in elementary school, I was taught that Christopher Columbus discovered North America after sailing the Ocean Blue in 1492. When I got to middle school they taught the story again, except this time in 1492 Christopher Columbus sailed the ocean blue looking for a new path to Asia and discovered not North America, but instead the Bahamas, and when he got there somehow thought he had made it to India. So now Christopher Columbus not only confused himself but confused everyone else into calling the people there Indians instead of Native Americans, which somehow still makes it into APUSH textbooks today. Finally, in high school I learn that Christopher Columbus sailed the Ocean Blue in 1492 looking for the middle passage, stumbled upon the Bahamas, met the Natives, started calling them Indians, and then enslaved and slaughtered them. In this situation Columbus arrived in the Bahamas without any previous background or experience with the type of culture style the natives employed. As he experienced their lifestyle for the first time, he labeled them as savages and many times spoke about being superior to them. Thinking about this story helped me understand how different people could view these statues based on their understanding or perception of the topic. 4th Grade me would not have understood why people wanted to tear down the statues of the man who discovered our home country (Brito). While now, having heard the full story, I can completely understand why they would not want to look up at a statue of a man who colonized and brutalized those living on their land.

paragraphs.body: This led me to wonder why people in Puerto Rico, which was a colony thanks to Columbus, have not torn down his statue Birth of a New World. Over the spring last year, I traveled to Puerto Rico with my family. We stayed for about a week and had a great time exploring the island. One of the most memorable things for me during the trip though was a 360-foot-tall statue of Christopher Columbus. My family and I were traveling through Arecibo to see a cave on the coast of the island called the Cueva del Indio when we first spotted the statue. The first thing I saw were the giant sails sticking out above the trees but as we got closer, I began to see the giant bronze hand and face. Once we had reached a portion of the road where the statue was fully visible, I looked up and thought to myself, who even is that supposed to be? The artist’s depiction of Columbus in some ways reminded me of an alien, his eyes appear soulless and the whole face is devoid of any expression. Along with this, the design of the clothing doesn’t resemble the breeches and cloak that I would associate with the late 1400s. Instead, the statue dons a more robe like appearance that I would really relate more to Jesus or some other religious figure. The statue is called Birth of the New World and the strangest thing to me, apart from its appearance, was its location. Puerto Rico is officially listed as a territory of the United States and the influence can be seen everywhere. About half the island speaks English, the official currency is the U.S. dollar, and the U.S. implemented the Jones-Shafroth Act in 1917 changing Puerto Rico’s government to a Representative Democracy (Cheatham and Roy). These are all similar tactics used in years prior by other colonizing countries like Spain who originally colonized Puerto Rico. One of the colonizers who sailed under the Spanish flag was Christopher Columbus who was the first European to step foot in Puerto Rico. I’ve always wondered what Puerto Ricans thought of Christopher Columbus knowing the history of their island since they technically wouldn’t exist if he hadn’t discovered it for the King and Queen of Spain. But on the other hand, their existence is also linked to the Taíno genocide which was a genocide committed against the natives of Puerto Rico by the Spanish Empire when they began colonizing. It’s interesting to consider how Puerto Ricans may feel looking up at this statue and I wonder if they may have mixed emotions about how it can represent either the discovery of their home or the genocide of the original Puerto Ricans. Unlike the Statue of Liberty, whose purpose might be a symbol of freedom or welcome to immigrants, or the Statue of Unity, which seeks to create pride about a unified India, this statue of Columbus seems out of place. It celebrates the “Birth of the New World,” which was only new to Europeans. It celebrates the birth of a colony in a place where a monument for the natives would seem more fit. Especially because of the history between the colonizers and the natives. After only 50 or so years of settling the Spanish had Commented [RO1]: https://www.nlm.nih.gov/nativevoices/timeline/170.ht ml of impending doom, the natives had their own defensive entourage of statues defending their shores? Would the symbolism and impact of a massive statue we see today hold the same belittling effect and cultural curiosity on Columbus during the 15th century that the Statue of Unity had on me today? Would the estimated 50,000 Taíno (Mathews and Wagenheim) people Commented [RO2]: https://www.britannica.com/place/Puerto-Rico/History who originally inhabited what we now know as Puerto Rico survived on to the 21st century or would Columbus have remained unfazed by the display leading to the same historical outcome we have today?
